guides.text: Let's first understand the goal. $I \to I$ means '$I$ [implication|implies] $I$' — if $I$ is true, then $I$ is true. Hardly controversial.
To prove an [implication|implication], we assume the left-hand side and try to reach the right-hand side. So let's assume $I$.

The symbol $\vdash$ is pronounced '[judgement|entails]'. Everything to its left is the **[context|context]** — a set of [proposition|propositions] we're allowed to take for granted. The right-hand side is what we can conclude from them.
So $\{I\} \vdash I$ reads: 'given $I$, we can deduce $I$'. Which is just a fancy way of saying: if you know $I$, you know $I$.

Let's pause and define a few terms. A **[judgement|judgement]** is a single line in a proof, written $\Gamma \vdash p$. It's a complete claim: "from these assumptions, I can prove this." The $\vdash$ symbol itself is called the **turnstile** — it separates what you know (left) from what you're proving (right). When we say $\Gamma$ [judgement|entails] $p$, we mean the stuff in $\Gamma$ is enough to logically force $p$ to be true. The formula on the right of $\vdash$ is the **[conclusion|conclusion]** — it's what you're actually trying to establish at that step. Every line you see in the proof area is a judgement, and the right-hand side of each line is that step's conclusion.

In our case: the context is $\{I\}$ (just the one [assumption|assumption]), the turnstile $\vdash$ says '[judgement|therefore]', and the [conclusion|conclusion] is $I$. A judgement, entailment, and conclusion — three names for three parts of the same line. Now let's finish the proof.

Now, '$A$ [implication|implies] $B$' **means** that $B$ follows from assuming $A$. We already have $\{I\} \vdash I$ at step 1. Use `->-intro` to discharge the [assumption|assumption] $I$ and close the proof. Its first argument is the [formula|formula] you want to discharge and the second is the step number:

unlocks.name: assume
unlocks.description: If you assume a proposition, you can deduce it — because you just said it's true.

unlocks.name: →-intro
unlocks.description: If from assuming $p$ you can reach $q$, then $p$ implies $q$ — and you can safely drop $p$ from the context.
